label: tetlock-est-Q6
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: estimate ONE sub-question in isolation. You see only the facts and
this sub-question; you must not analyze the broader dispute. Give a calibrated
probability and the few considerations doing the real work.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Sub-question to estimate (in isolation)

**What rate-design and revenue-allocation changes would bring the surcharge into Prop 218/26 compliance, and would implementing them require a new Prop 218 notice-and-hearing process or Prop 26/218 voter approval?**

What would have to be true: Would need to determine: (1) whether the district could re-derive the $0.85 figure purely from documented marginal cost (capping it near the 1.6× multiplier) and thereby cure the §6(b)(3) problem; (2) whether restricting surcharge-fund use to costs of service for the paying tier (or moving the low-income rebate program to general fund / grant / voter-approved tax revenue) cures the §6(b)(2) cross-subsidy problem; (3) whether any rate modification of this kind triggers a fresh Article XIII D §6(a) notice-and-majority-protest proceeding, and whether the existing 9% protest record has any preclusive or evidentiary relevance to a new proceeding.
text: {"key_considerations":["Curing §6(b)(3): the rate study's own 1.6x marginal-cost finding gives the district a defensible ceiling — re-deriving $0.85 (or a lower figure) strictly from that cost differential, stripping out 'conservation-incentive pricing' rationale, is a technically achievable fix and tracks Capistrano's core holding that tiered rates must track cost-of-service, not policy-driven demand suppression untethered from cost.","Curing §6(b)(2): redirecting the 55% low-income rebate spending off the surcharge-restricted fund (to general fund, grants, or a separately voter-approved tax) removes the cross-subsidy defect; retaining recycled-water debt service and conservation-education spending is more defensible since both plausibly relate to serving/managing the high-tier demand that generates the marginal cost.","Whether a fresh Art. XIII D §6(a) notice-and-majority-protest proceeding is legally *required* turns on whether the corrected rate counts as a 'new or increased' fee under Gov. Code §53750(h)(1) — a same-or-lower dollar charge with only a corrected cost study and revised fund-use might arguably not be an 'increase' requiring redo, but a wholesale re-derivation of methodology invalidated in litigation invites courts/agencies to treat it as a fresh 'imposition' out of caution (cf. Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil treating property-related water fees as fully subject to Prop 218 procedure).","Prop 26 voter-approval exposure is comparatively low if the fix succeeds: Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e)(2) exempts fees for a specific benefit not exceeding reasonable cost, and the agency bears the burden of proof — a properly cost-capped, non-redistributive surcharge should fit that exemption and avoid tax/2/3-vote characterization, whereas an uncured version risks exactly that reclassification.","Practical/administrative incentive is separate from legal necessity: agencies redesigning a successfully-challenged rate structure commonly re-run the full notice/protest process anyway (clean record, insulation from renewed suit, refund-exposure management under Plantier v. Ramona Mun. Water Dist.), even where the constitutional text might not strictly compel it for a decrease-only change."],"probability":60,"what_evidence_would_change_this":"Would shift materially with: (1) the text of Gov. Code §53750(h)(1)/(2) and any case law construing what counts as an 'increased' fee versus a mere reallocation/re-justification of an unchanged or lowered charge; (2) whether the district's enabling ordinance or the original Prop 218 notice already reserved authority to reallocate restricted-fund spending without new notice; (3) post-Capistrano appellate or trial-court guidance (including the Capistrano remand record itself) on whether a agency curing a §6(b) defect via recalculated cost study must redo the full §6(a) hearing or can do so by resolution; (4) confirmation of the current $0.85 figure's actual relation to the 1.6x marginal-cost ceiling (if $0.85 already sits at or below cost, a pure re-derivation may not even change the charged amount, weakening the case for a required new hearing).\""}

key_considerations: Curing §6(b)(3): the rate study's own 1.6x marginal-cost finding gives the district a defensible ceiling — re-deriving $0.85 (or a lower figure) strictly from that cost differential, stripping out 'conservation-incentive pricing' rationale, is a technically achievable fix and tracks Capistrano's core holding that tiered rates must track cost-of-service, not policy-driven demand suppression untethered from cost.

Curing §6(b)(2): redirecting the 55% low-income rebate spending off the surcharge-restricted fund (to general fund, grants, or a separately voter-approved tax) removes the cross-subsidy defect; retaining recycled-water debt service and conservation-education spending is more defensible since both plausibly relate to serving/managing the high-tier demand that generates the marginal cost.

Whether a fresh Art. XIII D §6(a) notice-and-majority-protest proceeding is legally *required* turns on whether the corrected rate counts as a 'new or increased' fee under Gov. Code §53750(h)(1) — a same-or-lower dollar charge with only a corrected cost study and revised fund-use might arguably not be an 'increase' requiring redo, but a wholesale re-derivation of methodology invalidated in litigation invites courts/agencies to treat it as a fresh 'imposition' out of caution (cf. Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil treating property-related water fees as fully subject to Prop 218 procedure).

Prop 26 voter-approval exposure is comparatively low if the fix succeeds: Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e)(2) exempts fees for a specific benefit not exceeding reasonable cost, and the agency bears the burden of proof — a properly cost-capped, non-redistributive surcharge should fit that exemption and avoid tax/2/3-vote characterization, whereas an uncured version risks exactly that reclassification.

Practical/administrative incentive is separate from legal necessity: agencies redesigning a successfully-challenged rate structure commonly re-run the full notice/protest process anyway (clean record, insulation from renewed suit, refund-exposure management under Plantier v. Ramona Mun. Water Dist.), even where the constitutional text might not strictly compel it for a decrease-only change.
data.what_evidence_would_change_this: Would shift materially with: (1) the text of Gov. Code §53750(h)(1)/(2) and any case law construing what counts as an 'increased' fee versus a mere reallocation/re-justification of an unchanged or lowered charge; (2) whether the district's enabling ordinance or the original Prop 218 notice already reserved authority to reallocate restricted-fund spending without new notice; (3) post-Capistrano appellate or trial-court guidance (including the Capistrano remand record itself) on whether a agency curing a §6(b) defect via recalculated cost study must redo the full §6(a) hearing or can do so by resolution; (4) confirmation of the current $0.85 figure's actual relation to the 1.6x marginal-cost ceiling (if $0.85 already sits at or below cost, a pure re-derivation may not even change the charged amount, weakening the case for a required new hearing)."
